start.text: Mr. Crane, the caretaker, polishes a spotless patch of the dusty front desk. 'Welcome to the Grand Palm Resort... or what's left of it. You kids shouldn't be here after dark. The Phantom doesn't like guests.'

choices.text: 'The ledger says supplies were billed to Room 217 — which doesn't exist.'
'Explain the deed in your safe. The one selling YOUR resort to YOU.'

ghost.text: 'Glows green as swamp gas, wails like a busted pipe organ. It's scared off every buyer I've shown the property to. Some nights I see it drifting across the grounds toward the old boiler room...'

janitor.text: 'Gus? Harmless enough. Keeps to himself. Though he does spend an awful lot of time in that locked closet of his. Lost his key recently, or so he claims.'

sale.text: Crane's eye twitches. 'Between us? If the resort doesn't sell soon, the bank takes everything. Though with the hauntings, the price keeps dropping... and dropping...' He smiles faintly, then catches himself.

ledger_press.text: Crane goes very still. 'You read the ledger? Nosy little... ahem. A clerical error, nothing more. Room 217 was — water damaged. Yes. Don't go poking around the boiler room, it isn't SAFE.' He didn't mention the boiler room before you did.

deed_press.text: Crane's polishing rag stops mid-circle. For three full seconds the lobby is very, very quiet. 'You opened my safe.' He smiles like a crack spreading across ice. 'Enjoy the resort while you can, children. Its NEW OWNER may not be so... hospitable.'

start.text: Janitor Gus stops raking and eyes you warily over the dead leaves. 'Resort's closed, kids. Nothin' out here but weeds and bad memories.'

choices.text: Show him the can of glowing paint

ghost.text: 'Ain't no ghost.' He grips the rake tighter. 'Just drafts, bad wiring, and folks seein' what they're told to see. Now scram before you get hurt. Or... before somethin' hurts you.'

shed.text: 'Toolshed? Dropped my maintenance key in there weeks back. Too dark to find it, and the boss won't spring for a new bulb. If you fish it out, don't go usin' it on my closet, y'hear?'

confront.text: You hold up the can of LUMO-GLOW paint. Gus goes white as a — well, you know. 'Where'd you— okay, OKAY! I bought that paint, but only 'cause MR. CRANE told me to! Said it was for renovatin' the pool mural! Six cans of ghost-green paint for a mural, and him prowlin' the boiler room every night — I ain't stupid, kids, and I ain't takin' the fall!'

truth.text: Gus nods grimly. 'Catch him in the act and I'll back your story. He keeps his getup down in the boiler room, under my closet — and take my spare passkey to his OFFICE. Whatever that man's really planning, the paperwork's up those lobby stairs. And kids... lately I hear BANGING from behind his office wall. Walls ain't supposed to bang.'

choices.text: 'Thanks, Gus. Gang — we've got an office to search!'

spare_key.text: 'Figured you'd ask.' Gus fishes a brass passkey from his overalls. 'Spare passkey — opens the office at the top of the lobby stairs. And listen... some nights I hear BANGING behind his office wall. Walls ain't supposed to bang.'

start.text: You yank the lever! The Phantom Guest — mid-lunge at the Scooby Snack — is snatched into the laundry net and hauled kicking toward the ceiling! 'RELEASE ME, MEDDLERS! THE PHANTOM CURSES YOU ALLLL!'

whodunit.text: The phantom writhes in the net, sheet still on. Velma adjusts her glasses: 'Hold on, gang — a proper unmasking needs a proper ACCUSATION. Think about the evidence... WHO is under that sheet?'

choices.text: 'Like, it's obviously Janitor Gus! It's HIS closet!'
'It's George Palm! He wants the resort for himself!'
'It's Mr. Crane. The ledger, the paint invoice, the boiler room — it all fits.'

wrong_gus.text: Daphne glances out the boiler-room grate... Gus is STILL up on the grounds, raking leaves in the moonlight. He waves back, sarcastically. Velma sighs: 'Also, the phantom is a full head taller than Gus. The boots were size 12 — Gus wears galoshes. Try again.'

wrong_ghost.text: Shaggy and Scooby are already halfway up a steam pipe! Inside the net, the 'ghost' rolls its eyes and checks its wristwatch. Velma taps her foot: 'Ghosts do not wear GRAND PALM STAFF watches, you two. Get down here.'

reveal.text: Velma yanks off the glowing sheet... 'MR. CRANE?!' The caretaker sags in the net as George Palm III steps forward with the deed draft. 'Scare off every buyer, bankrupt the estate, then buy paradise for pennies — with MY family's money troubles as cover. And the Wail-o-Phone, Ambrose? Really?' Crane thrashes: 'And I would have gotten away with it too, if it weren't for you meddling kids, the mangy mutt, and the HEIR I HAD TIED TO A CHAIR!' Scooby: 'ROOBY-ROOBY-ROO!'

wrong_george.text: A voice from the stairwell: 'I can hear you, you know.' George Palm III walks in, STILL picking clothesline out of his hair. Velma: 'The phantom tied George up because George refused to sell. Victims rarely haunt themselves. Think about who profits when the price hits rock bottom...'

start.text: You slice the clothesline and the young man bursts free, spitting out a sock. 'FINALLY! George Palm — the third. My grandfather BUILT this place, and I came back to reopen it, not sell it! Two nights ago I followed the "phantom" up here... and the phantom locked me in my own grandfather's secret suite!'

choices.text: 'Did you see who it was under the sheet?'
'Wait — reopen it? Crane says it has to sell.'

motive.text: 'Sell it? To WHO?' George's jaw tightens. 'The only offer on the table is some lowball buyer nobody's ever met. As the heir I was about to refuse it and reopen the Grand Palm myself. Funny thing — the hauntings started the same week I announced that.'

choices.text: 'Did you see who it was under the sheet?'

witness.text: 'Better. When he shoved me in here, his sheet snagged the door and lifted.' George looks grim. 'Silver hair. Staff watch. And a keyring that jingled like sleigh bells — the master ring only ONE person at this resort carries. I'll testify to the police, but you'll need to catch him in the act.'

choices.text: 'Then we build a trap. Stay hidden until you hear the net spring!'
